Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the concept of 'unit' in unit testing, especially as we navigate the complexities of refactoring. It's fascinating how the definition has shifted from a focus on meaningful behavior to often being tied to classes or methods. 🤔 

I believe we should be testing at the level of functional behavior—grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This approach not only protects us during refactoring but also keeps our tests relevant and effective. 

I'm genuinely curious about your experiences with this. What patterns have worked for your teams, especially at scale? Let's share insights and help each other refine our testing strategies! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

deltas.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

actual.review_text: Transparency should be implemented at company level and it should be presented as brackets, not individual salaries. Based on personal merit and impact on company any individual progresses towards the higher part of the bracket until promoted. The first place to start with this transparency should be in job ads though, many times the lack of this transparency encourages personal statistics made by asking friends who don't really do the job you're applying for or searching inaccurate websites causing many people to make bad decisions of either asking too much or too little in interviews. Transparency can also be a motivating factor, to know what one could earn more by doing some specific type of development can make some people have clear career paths early on, provided they have the dedication, ability and if it's also interesting to them.

deltas.product_description:
“How do some people do so much in one life?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” Those who try never really lose.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes perseverance and continuous effort. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

I left him with a question: what is one area you have thought “not for me” that deserves one more honest try this month? Treat every stumble as the ant’s slip or the diver’s empty hand, not as the final verdict.
#growthmindset #grit #parenting #entrepreneurship #hospitality #learning #resilience

From perfect attendance in school to earning patents and thriving at Microsoft, I’ve embraced this mindset across my journey. Each small win becomes the floor for the next challenge. This weekend, I took it a step further by shadowing a renowned event planner to elevate our hospitality venture. 🚀 If you want to try this approach, pick one behavior, set a small version to maintain, and track your progress. Let’s keep pushing those boundaries! #CompoundingEffort #IntrinsicMotivation #ContinuousGrowth

deltas.product_description:
How I built a habit that prevents quitting? 

I developed this as a child. Protect yesterday’s win, then raise the ceiling.

It began in school. Two months without an absence became a quarter, then a year. From sixth through tenth grade I did not miss a day. The streak made the choice each morning. Protect the win. Add one.

That rule shaped bigger moves. I chose engineering and left home after tenth grade to build my own path. Received first US patent, then added more. Same pattern reflected in other aspects. Thirteen years of clean driving record. Sixteen years at Microsoft with strong peer relationships and consistent recognition. The goal was not tenure. The goal was to continuously raise yesterday’s baseline. If 30 pushups is the edge, I do 31. One extra rep turns a ceiling into a floor.

Outside of work, Hospitality Entrepreneurship tested the pattern. Seven years ago we (Rahil and I) converted our garage into a studio and hosted our first guests. Then more condos. Then a vineyard stay. Now we are building Kēsar Vineyards and Events a specialized multi-day wedding venue. Same rule. Keep the last win alive. Stretch it slightly. Then add one to create new personal baseline. This weekend we drove 5hrs to shadow a renowned event and wedding planner during a live event and study the craft up close. New input. New higher standard.

I’m sharing because many of us want a habit that prevents quitting. This is mine. I treat yesterday’s small win as the floor. I refuse to break the floor. Then I add one. I rely on my system: small streaks that become identity, and identity that makes the next action non-negotiable. Tiny on any given day. Transformative across a season.

If you want to try it for 30 days: pick one behavior that matters, set the smallest version you can keep on your worst day, track one visible receipt, protect the floor, then add one. #CompoundingEffort #TheStreakIsTheStrategy #IntrinsicMotivation

deltas.product_description:
I'm a little late to the party, but I finally put AI to the test on a real coding project.

The project is a support tool for our development team, so low risk if something went wrong. I asked Claude to improve some WPF canvas code by adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel, along with click events.

It took several iterations. Sometimes the code didn’t compile, sometimes I fixed the bugs myself, and sometimes I just made tweaks by hand (which was faster than re-prompting AI). In the end, the antenna looked good. The code was a bit messy, but then again, the original codebase wasn't exactly pretty either, so maybe the AI was just following our conventions. 

My takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- Great for exploration. It surfaced APIs and language features I hadn’t seen before.

- Not great for polish. Expect to refactor if you care about clean code.

- Less useful when you already know the solution. Typing has never been the bottleneck.

The biggest time saver was discovering parts of the WPF Canvas API I didn’t know. Normally, I’d have learned this more slowly through searches and documentation. Still, I believe it's important to take time to study those new concepts that AI introduces us to. Otherwise we aren't really qualified to evaluate the quality of what AI produces.

For me, AI fit neatly into the early phase of problem-solving: piecing together a solution while expanding my knowledge. Once I understood the problem and knew what "good" looked like, it was easier to take over, clean things up, and bring the code up to company standards. Once the solution became clear, prompting AI further felt less like engineering and more like pulling a slot machine lever.

I know I still have a lot to learn in this new programming paradigm. How does this line up with your experience?

deltas.product_description:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.

deltas.product_description:
Virtual Network Routing Appliance ("Routing Appliance") Public Preview!

During Ignite, I posted about exciting developments in building a high-performance, native appliance in Azure that will help customers achieve low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need of a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). Today I'm happy to announce that we have released a public preview of this feature. I hope you get a chance to try it out and we are looking to build many more exciting capabilities on top of this new technology.

NVAs have been the backbone of complex Azure networking topologies where customers needed to route traffic through a central point. While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point. NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps. You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

Read More: 

Thanks to Ashish Bhargava, Mario Lopez, Mohamed Hassan, Jackson Crewe, Nitin Goel, Pan Wang, Anshu Verma, Arjun Patel, Rishabh Tewari, Neeraj Motwani, Avijit Gupta, Damini Singal, Michal Zygmunt, Deepak Bansal

deltas.product_description:
As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements. This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space. While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together. It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound. 

Features of a network security perimeter include: 
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations. 
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter. 
* Access logs for audit and compliance. 
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: 

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:

That's the leadership ratio for a well-shaped, well-led software development team.

Set this ratio as your target, and then fix, as soon as humanly possible, anything that arises that might keep you from it.

#softwaredevelopment #leadershipcraftsmanship #team

prediction.review_text:
As I reflect on team dynamics, I can't help but think about the importance of empowering each member rather than micromanaging. 🚀 Here are 5 signs of a toxic micromanager, inspired by a recent discussion:

1. Inviting everyone to meetings without purpose.
2. Telling team members what to do instead of trusting their expertise.
3. Favoring certain individuals over the whole team.
4. Disregarding personal time and expecting immediate responses.
5. Making promises to clients without team consensus.

Let's strive for a culture that values collaboration and respects each other's time and contributions. How do you foster a positive team environment? #Leadership #Teamwork #Empowerment

actual.review_text: Ognjen Sobajic s druge strane, opet da ne bude da ga samo kudimo, uvek mi se svidjalo kako ocas posla napravi dobru prezentaciju gde je sve odlicno objasnjeno sa veoma lepim vizualizacijama. Verovatno je koristio neke napredne AI tool-ove jos tada.

deltas.product_description:
5 dokaza da je Rajder iz Patrolnih šapa toksični mikromenadžer:

1. Ne vrednuje tuđe vreme. Na sastanke saziva sve članove tima, umesto samo one koji će učestvovati u akciji.

2. Govori drugima šta da rade, umesto da njima to prepusti. Kao da Rabl ne zna šta treba da radi kašikom bagera ili Skaj kukom helikoptera.

3. Favorizuje određene članove tima. U ogromnom broju akcija učestvuju Čejs i Skaj, dok Maršal dođe na red u svakoj 10. epizodi (čekali smo pet dana da čujemo koja je Maršalova rečenica kad ga Rajder prozove)

4. Ne poštuje slobodno vreme članova svog tima. Često ih zove čak i po mraku, očekujući da se oni momentalno odazovu. To stvara ogroman stres kod Šapa.

5. Daje klijentima obećanja bez prethodne saglasnosti članova svog tima. Svakom ko ga nazove kaže da odmah dolaze i da će biti rešeno, čime Šapama ne ostavlja nimalo prostora za pregovore i stalno ih baca u vatru.

(Do temperature je, nije do mene)

prediction.review_text:
Open spaces are often touted as the future of collaboration, but my experience tells a different story. After 15 years in IT, I’ve seen how these environments can stifle communication rather than enhance it. 🎧 I find myself yearning for better headphones and a quiet space to focus, rather than engaging with colleagues. 

The reality is, collaboration thrives on trust and openness, not just proximity. If the organizational culture is closed, removing walls won’t solve anything. Let’s prioritize building a culture of communication and trust over just rearranging furniture. 

#TeamDynamics #Collaboration #OrganizationalCulture

actual.review_text: U principu tačno. Teško je ukomponovati da ljudi koji rade zajedno baš sede jedni pored drugih, možda i nemoguče. Danas radim sa jednim kolegom i saradjujem dosta, a za dve nedelje će mi glavni saradnik biti neko potuno drugi.

deltas.product_description:
Zašto je open space sr…nje? Ili u prevodu, kada mogu da dobijem bolje slušalice?

Petnaest godina sam vodio IT kompanije.
Deset godina pre toga radio sam u njima, kao inženjer.
Za sve to vreme nagledao sam se različitih kancelarijskih formata, ali jedna tema se nike menjala, open space. Uvek je imao istu priču: povećaće kolaboraciju, ljudi će više razgovarati, biće otvoreniji jedni prema drugima i efikasnost rada na projektima će porasti.

Ali stvarnost je potpuno drugačija. Da li mogu da dobijem bolje slušalice i mikrofon koji izoluje buku, da li mogu da se premestim u drugu kancelariju, da li mogu intervju sa klijentom da odradim od kuće, potreban mi je fokus. 

U tim prostorima gde bi trebalo da se „svi čuju“, ljudi se sve manje slušaju.
Sede jedni pored drugih, a ne kažu ni „dobro jutro“. Kada sam prvi put to video davne 2008.godine, nisam mogao da verujem. Sedim u open space kancelariji za pedesetak ljudi, u drugom gradu, i gledam, ulaze jedan po jedan, i samo re retki javljaju jedni drugima. Oni koji sede jedni do drugih, naravno, najšešće.
Neki ne znaju imena svojih kolega, niti imaju predstavu ko uopšte radi sa kojim tehnologijama i na kojim projektima.
Zamišljen kao otvoren, zajednički prostor, open space se pretvorio u zatvorenost, u prostor gde se svako brani od drugih svojim slušalicama, ekranima i pogledom u pod.

Menadžment voli da kaže da zidovi prave barijere.
Istina je da barijere prave ljudi.
Ako ti je organizaciona kultura zatvorena, ako nema poverenja, ako ljudi ne osećaju potrebu da se diskutuju jedni sa drugima, skidanje zidova neće promeniti ništa osim troškova za molera.
I tu leži ironija: kada saradnja ne postoji, nije problem u rasporedu stolova, nego u onome kako vodiš firmu, i kakvu organizacionu kulturu živi ta kompanija. Ono što sam naučio kroz karijeru i kroz istraživanje u pripremi disertacije je da je organizaciona kultura kao voda. Ukoliko je ti ne oblikuješ, ne menadžuješ, sa stavom, “ma sve će to da se desi samo od sebe”, organizaciona kultura nadje svoj put, i svoj način da se uoblikuje. Često, ne u ono što menadžment kompanije želi.

Zašto se i dalje guramo u open space, zašto se to prodaje kao „rešenje za budućnost rada“, i zašto menadžment uporno brani koncept koji očigledno ne funkcioniše?
Možda zato što je lakše meriti kvadrate nego poverenje.
Ali pravo pitanje je: da li menadžment zaista veruje da ljudi rade bolje kada sede zajedno, ili je to samo mit koji niko ne sme da prizna da ne funkcioniše? Ja nisam smeo, priznajem.

prediction.review_text:
As the founder of CodingRippler, I've always focused on generating inbound interest for our services. However, with the emergence of LLMs like ChatGPT and others, I'm intrigued by how this landscape is evolving. Are we being intentional about our visibility when our ideal customers search for specific challenges? 🤔 

I believe boutique firms like ours have a unique advantage in providing niche solutions to complex problems. This is where we can truly shine by being strategic about our relevance in these conversations. 

I’d love to hear from you all! How are you adapting your strategies in this changing environment? Let’s share insights and learn from each other’s experiences. #AI #Automation #ProServ #LLMs

actual.review_text: Absolutely Ben Edwards I think boutique firms can really shine here because they can give very niche, specific answers to unique problems

deltas.product_description:

Are you getting any interest via LLMs like ChatGPT, Grok, Claude or Gemini (AI google search)?

If so... are you intentional about appearing when ICP prospects search for specific problems or challenges? 

It's another little area I'm thinking boutique Pro Serv firms should have the upper hand on the bigger firms on... because you should have really niche & specific answers to really niche problems & challenges... but it's just a hypothesis with no data & very little anecdotal evidence yet!

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on a common misconception in the automation space that I’ve fallen into myself. Many agencies push automation as a product, but in reality, this often leads to increased costs before delivering any real value. 

The true need for automation arises when manual processes become bottlenecks—when scaling becomes painful and error-prone. That’s when automation transitions from a 'nice-to-have' to a crucial enabler of growth. 

At CodingRippler, we prioritize building automation solutions where the pain is most acute, ensuring they emerge organically as teams scale. 

Instead of selling automation, let’s discover where it truly makes sense. Curious if your team has hit that point? Let’s explore together! 💡 

Want to see how other teams identified the right moments to automate and the impact it created? Check out our case studies! 🔗

actual.review_text: Absolutely, Oleksandra Bielova , I completely agree. Many teams take up automation just to show progress, even when it’s not really needed. Often it’s more about keeping leadership happy than solving a real problem.

deltas.product_description:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, I’ve been guilty of it too.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

So instead of trying to sell automation, we build it where the pain is real, where teams are already doing the work, and scaling it manually no longer makes sense.

At CodingRippler, that’s how we think about automation,
not as a product to push, but as a capability that emerges naturally when scaling requires it.

Maybe automation shouldn’t be sold, it should be discovered.

prediction.review_text:
Today, I proudly celebrate 5 years at Microsoft! 🎉 It's hard to believe that my journey began with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology from WVU in 2014, and now I stand as a Senior Software Engineer, deeply involved in Azure Government. I owe a tremendous amount to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which was my gateway into programming and set me on this incredible path. A special thanks to Deloitte for equipping me with essential soft skills and showcasing the power of collaboration in tech. 

I am especially grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for their tuition assistance, allowing me to earn my master's degree without debt. My experience as a Field Artillery Officer has instilled in me invaluable leadership and resilience that no classroom could replicate. 

To all the mentors and leaders—both civilian and military—who have guided me along the way, your support has been instrumental in shaping my career. I can't express enough how much your influence means to me. 

This journey is a testament to hard work, clear goals, and the importance of mentorship. Here’s to the next chapter! #Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #Gratitude #VeteransInTech #Mentorship

actual.review_text: Jeffery Galbraith people like you and the Army taught me a lot. Forced me to be better.

deltas.product_description:
Celebrating 5 Years at Microsoft 

If you had told me when I graduated from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology that I would one day be a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft with a Master of Science in Software Engineering, I might not have believed you. Yet here I am in 2025, reflecting on an incredible journey filled with growth, opportunity, and gratitude.

First, I owe so much to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which gave me my start in programming and changed the trajectory of my career. A heartfelt thanks also to Deloitte, where I learned invaluable soft skills and the true power of an inclusive and collaborative organization.

I’m deeply grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for enabling me to earn my master’s degree debt-free through their exceptional tuition assistance programs. If you’re looking for the best deal in education, this is it!

My time as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer in the Army National Guard has taught me leadership and resilience in ways that no classroom or corporate training ever could.

To all the incredible civilian and military leaders and mentors I’ve had the privilege of learning from over the years—your guidance and support have truly shaped who I am today. Your impact on my life has been profound, and I am forever grateful.

This journey is a testament to the power of hard work and having clear goals. It truly is the American dream.

I’m incredibly thankful, humbled, and blessed to be where I am today. Here’s to the next chapter!

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #ArmyNationalGuard #Gratitude #AmericanDream

deltas.product_description:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, I’ve been guilty of it too.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

So instead of trying to sell automation, we build it where the pain is real, where teams are already doing the work, and scaling it manually no longer makes sense.

At CodingRippler, that’s how we think about automation,
not as a product to push, but as a capability that emerges naturally when scaling requires it.

Your support has been invaluable, and I truly respect the impact you've made in the open-source community. 🌟

Solo.io is in a fantastic position with its innovative K8S gateway and mTLS solutions. I’m excited to see how these advancements will shape the future of cloud-native technology.

As I transition to Edera, I look forward to addressing the critical gaps in K8S workload isolation. Let's continue pushing the boundaries of what's possible together! 💪 #Kubernetes #OpenSource #CloudNative

actual.review_text: It was fantastic to work with you in the Istio community Ben! Best of luck at Edera

deltas.product_description:
This week marks my last at solo.io - it's been a great two years, I've learned a *ton*, enjoyed working with some very smart people, accepted a maintainership for a relatively mature and complex open-source project (Istio), gotten to do lots of interesting low-level Linux stuff, developed a very strong appreciation for both Rust/Tokio, and new respect for *anyone* that takes on the challenge of writing spec-complete ` implementations :P

Extreme shoutout to Lin Sun who gave me this (and many other) opportunities and has been an incredible mentor and boss - Lin is a massive asset to the open-source community as a whole, and someone I genuinely respect and trust on a personal level as well.

solo.io is in a remarkably strong position in the market now - the most powerful OSS K8S gateway offering (kgateway) + the fastest, cheapest and most invisible way to secure cluster traffic with mTLS (Istio Ambient) - and a ton of great people.

It's been great working with you all, I'm off to Edera to help plug the well-known gaps in K8S workload isolation!

deltas.product_description:
Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling US Army

prediction.review_text:
As a software engineer, I often find myself caught between the ideal of building scalable systems and the reality of delivering quickly. 🚀 It's easy to get lost in the details and over-engineer solutions, but sometimes, we just need to kick off a project and iterate as we go. 

When working on proof of concepts, speed and agility are key. The goal is to validate ideas swiftly, allowing us to refine and scale later if the concept proves successful. Real-world challenges often demand practical solutions over perfect designs. 

Let's embrace the iterative process and focus on the impact we can create together! 💡 How do you balance speed and scalability in your projects? 

#SoftwareDevelopment #Agile #ProofOfConcept #TeamDynamics #TrustInRemoteWork #PurposeDrivenDiscussions

actual.review_text:
When I see my own code too after some time too 

I think it's because we don't have context so it's mentally hard to read code we haven't recently written. 

Most of the time there is a reason things are the way they are and after some time working on the product I come to rediscover it.

deltas.product_description:
Some Thoughts on Development

As a developer, I care a lot about the scalability of the systems I build and like to think long-term.

But that’s not always the case. Sometimes, I realize I just need to kick it off and get it done. There are times when I don’t need to over-engineer the code or create long-term solutions. Real-world problems can be quite different from ideal system designs.

For instance, if we’re building a proof of concept, it might be more important to deliver quickly than to ensure the system can handle millions of users. We can always refine and scale it later if the concept proves successful.

deltas.product_description:
Some Thoughts on Development

As a developer, I care a lot about the scalability of the systems I build and like to think long-term.

But that’s not always the case. Sometimes, I realize I just need to kick it off and get it done. There are times when I don’t need to over-engineer the code or create long-term solutions. Real-world problems can be quite different from ideal system designs.

For instance, if we’re building a proof of concept, it might be more important to deliver quickly than to ensure the system can handle millions of users. We can always refine and scale it later if the concept proves successful.

prediction.review_text:
As I wrap up my time at solo.io, I want to take a moment to reflect on the incredible journey I've had over the past two years. Working on the Istio project has not only been a technical challenge but also a rewarding experience that has allowed me to collaborate with some of the most talented individuals in the open-source community. 

A huge shoutout to Lin Sun for being an exceptional mentor and leader. Your guidance has been instrumental in my growth, and I truly appreciate the opportunities you've provided me. It's mentors like you who make a significant impact in our field and inspire others to give back to the community. 

Solo.io is in a fantastic position with its innovative K8S gateway and mTLS solutions, and I’m excited to see how these advancements will continue to shape the future of cloud-native technology. 

As I transition to Edera, I look forward to tackling the challenges of K8S workload isolation and continuing to contribute to our vibrant community. Thank you all for the support and collaboration—let’s keep pushing the boundaries together! 💪 #Kubernetes #OpenSource #CloudNative

actual.review_text: It was fantastic to work with you in the Istio community Ben! Best of luck at Edera

deltas.product_description:
This week marks my last at solo.io - it's been a great two years, I've learned a *ton*, enjoyed working with some very smart people, accepted a maintainership for a relatively mature and complex open-source project (Istio), gotten to do lots of interesting low-level Linux stuff, developed a very strong appreciation for both Rust/Tokio, and new respect for *anyone* that takes on the challenge of writing spec-complete ` implementations :P

Extreme shoutout to Lin Sun who gave me this (and many other) opportunities and has been an incredible mentor and boss - Lin is a massive asset to the open-source community as a whole, and someone I genuinely respect and trust on a personal level as well.

solo.io is in a remarkably strong position in the market now - the most powerful OSS K8S gateway offering (kgateway) + the fastest, cheapest and most invisible way to secure cluster traffic with mTLS (Istio Ambient) - and a ton of great people.

It's been great working with you all, I'm off to Edera to help plug the well-known gaps in K8S workload isolation!

deltas.product_description:
Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling US Army

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the concept of 'unit' in unit testing, especially as we navigate the complexities of refactoring. It's fascinating how the definition has shifted from a focus on meaningful behavior to often being tied to classes or methods. 🤔 

I believe we should be testing at the level of functional behavior—grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This approach not only protects us during refactoring but also keeps our tests relevant and effective. 

I'm genuinely curious about your experiences with this. What patterns have worked for your teams, especially at scale? Let's share insights and help each other refine our testing strategies! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

deltas.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

These colleagues have been instrumental in my growth and development within the company, and I've learned so much from them

That's why I'm reaching out to you, my trusted network. I was hoping that perhaps someone out there might be able to offer a referral for these amazing engineers. They are not only incredibly talented, but also wonderful individuals to work with. They have a passion for their craft that is unmatched, and their expertise has made a significant impact on our team.

So, if you or someone you know is looking to hire talented and driven engineers, please don't hesitate to reach out. I would be forever grateful for your help in finding a new home for these amazing individuals. Together, we can help them land on their feet and continue to thrive in their careers. Thank you in advance for your help!

Some additional info:
- These engineers are based in Vancouver or in the US
- Focused more on backend development though have full stack experience

Edit: Thanks for all the help everyone! Please keep them coming!

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the evolution of the term 'unit' in unit testing, and it's fascinating how our understanding has shifted over the years. When Kent Beck introduced Test-Driven Development (TDD), a 'unit' was about meaningful behavior—something that truly reflects what the system does. However, it seems that the definition has morphed, especially with the influence of the London school of TDD, where 'unit' often translates to 'class' or 'method.' 🤔

I believe we should focus on testing at the level of functional behavior, grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This approach ensures that when we refactor, our tests remain relevant and effective, acting as a safety net rather than a cage. 

I'm genuinely curious to hear from my network: How have you navigated this tension in your teams? What patterns have worked for you, especially when scaling? Let's share our insights and refine our testing strategies together! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

deltas.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

🌟 Job seekers, are you ready to take control of your interview process? With the current job market favoring candidates, interviews are not just about showcasing your skills—they're a crucial opportunity to assess a company's culture and values. 

I recently spoke with several career experts, and they shared some critical red flags to watch out for during interviews. These insights can help you determine whether a company will genuinely support your growth and align with your values. 

Check out the latest article on Business Insider for actionable tips that can empower your job search. Let's navigate this journey together and make informed decisions that elevate our careers! 💼 #jobsearch #interviewtips #companyculture #careerdevelopment

actual.review_text: Vincent Hall PhD That's an intetesting point, but the most technical experience I've earned was in places where I worked for at least 3, or close to 3 years, maybe that applies more to some jobs than others. My comment was focused on starting a professional collaboration and at that time the end was already on my potential employer's mind, and what assurance I can provide for a long stay. I think that's a bad way to start down that road and proves a lack of trust in my general work ethic. That's the red flag.

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the evolution of the term 'unit' in unit testing, and it's fascinating how our understanding has shifted over the years. When Kent Beck introduced Test-Driven Development (TDD), a 'unit' was about meaningful behavior—something that truly reflects what the system does. However, it seems that the definition has morphed, especially with the influence of the London school of TDD, where 'unit' often translates to 'class' or 'method.' 🤔

I believe we should focus on testing at the level of functional behavior, grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This approach ensures that when we refactor, our tests remain relevant and effective, acting as a safety net rather than a cage. 

I'm genuinely curious to hear from my network: How have you navigated this tension in your teams? What patterns have worked for you, especially when scaling? Let's share our insights and refine our testing strategies together! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

deltas.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

deltas.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

prediction.review_text:
During a recent ride with my six-year-old, he asked, "How do some people do so much in one life?" Instead of diving into productivity hacks, I shared my favorite Hindi poem, "Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti." This poem beautifully illustrates the essence of perseverance—like the ant that keeps climbing despite slipping or the diver who searches for pearls in deep waters. 🌊 

This mindset is not just a personal lesson; it's what drives innovation at Microsoft and fuels our hospitality venture, Kēsar Vineyards and Events. Each challenge we face is an opportunity to learn and grow. 

So, I ask you all: what’s one area you’ve thought, "not for me" that deserves another honest try this month? Let’s embrace the journey together and remember, every stumble is just part of the process. 💪 #GrowthMindset #Resilience #DiversityAndInclusion #AIasCollaboration

actual.review_text:
Love Love Love it Swati !! So beautifully articulated. We wouldn't have light bulbs if Thomas Edison decided to give up. And many many such examples. 

Love these two quotes by him. 
"Genius is one percent inspiration and ninety-nine percent perspiration" and "I have not failed. I've just found 10,000 ways that won't work"

deltas.product_description:
“How do some people do so much in one life?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” Those who try never really lose.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes perseverance and continuous effort. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

deltas.product_description:
Yesterday marked the final day of my Explore (SWE + TPM) Internship at Xbox!

As a TPM, I led partner studio discussions, authored a feature spec, and collaborated across time zones with a designer to produce Figma designs.

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #CommunityBuilding #Networking

actual.review_text: Love this Saritha, I think I need to learn how to play as well. I prefer deep connections and real conversations!

deltas.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

deltas.product_description:
“How do some people do so much in one life?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” Those who try never really lose.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes perseverance and continuous effort. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

I left him with a question: what is one area you have thought “not for me” that deserves one more honest try this month? Treat every stumble as the ant’s slip or the diver’s empty hand, not as the final verdict.
#growthmindset #grit #parenting #entrepreneurship #hospitality #learning #resilience

prediction.review_text:
During a recent ride with my six-year-old, he asked, "How do some people do so much in one life?" Instead of diving into productivity hacks, I shared my favorite Hindi poem, "Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti." This poem beautifully illustrates the essence of perseverance—like the ant that keeps climbing despite slipping or the diver who searches for pearls in deep waters. 🌊 

This mindset is not just a personal lesson; it’s what drives innovation at Microsoft and fuels our hospitality venture, Kēsar Vineyards and Events. Each challenge we face is an opportunity to learn and grow, reminding us that every stumble is just part of the journey. 

So, I ask you all: what’s one area you’ve thought, "not for me" that deserves another honest try this month? Let’s embrace the journey together and remember, every stumble is just part of the process. 💪 #GrowthMindset #Resilience #AIasCollaboration #Storytelling #Perseverance

actual.review_text:
Love Love Love it Swati !! So beautifully articulated. We wouldn't have light bulbs if Thomas Edison decided to give up. And many many such examples. 

Love these two quotes by him. 
"Genius is one percent inspiration and ninety-nine percent perspiration" and "I have not failed. I've just found 10,000 ways that won't work"

deltas.product_description:
“How do some people do so much in one life?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” Those who try never really lose.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes perseverance and continuous effort. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

I left him with a question: what is one area you have thought “not for me” that deserves one more honest try this month? Treat every stumble as the ant’s slip or the diver’s empty hand, not as the final verdict.
#growthmindset #grit #parenting #entrepreneurship #hospitality #learning #resilience

prediction.review_text:
As someone deeply invested in fostering authentic connections within the tech community, I’m excited to see Mahjong emerging as a vibrant networking platform for women in tech! 🌟 This isn't just a passing trend; it's a transformative movement towards genuine interactions. Gone are the days of cold intros and badge scans—this is about laughter, storytelling, and meaningful conversations over tiles.

With Mahjong events surging by 180% across the U.S., it’s clear that we crave spaces where we can express ourselves and collaborate authentically. Founders are swapping traditional VC mixers for Mahjong Nights, and startups are hosting tournaments that feel more like community celebrations than business meetings.

This approach to networking is not just refreshing; it's essential! If you’re building something impactful or simply seeking a sense of belonging, don’t underestimate the power of the mahjong table. The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator could be sitting right across from you.

Let’s redefine networking together, one tile at a time! Who’s ready for a game? 

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #CommunityBuilding #Networking #TrustAndTransparency

actual.review_text: Love this Saritha, I think I need to learn how to play as well. I prefer deep connections and real conversations!

deltas.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

735 applicants for a single job? 🤯 This staggering number underscores the inefficiencies in our recruitment processes. With the average resume being reviewed for just 11 seconds, it's no wonder that only about 2% make it to the interview stage. 

As a software engineer and mentor, I've seen firsthand how critical it is to rethink our approach to hiring. We need solutions that not only streamline the process but also ensure we’re finding the right talent for the right roles. 

That’s why I’m excited about CLEVERHIRE.co, launching this summer! This platform aims to transform how job seekers and hiring managers connect, making recruitment more effective and inclusive. 

What innovative strategies have you seen or implemented to improve recruitment efficiency? Let’s share insights and drive change together! #RecruitmentSolutions #Hiring #Innovation

735 applicants for 1 job. Or clicks. (Calming down all who only look at the headline).

How could a company reasonably sort through all of these applicants?

Average resume is reviewed for 11 seconds 
~2% of applicants get an interview (14.7 of 735)

prediction.review_text:
I recently had an interview experience that really stood out to me, not for the right reasons. The interviewer walked in with an air of superiority and immediately asked, 'What’s the difference between Java and C++?' After I explained some key differences, he dismissed my response with a loud 'WRONG!' and insisted they were the same. This interaction left me reflecting on the importance of respectful dialogue in our industry. 

As I step into my new role at Microsoft, I’m reminded of the impact that our communication style can have on team dynamics and individual growth. Have you had similar experiences? How do you navigate challenging interviews? #CareerDevelopment #Mentorship #Microsoft

actual.review_text: It's better to find out before you get hired than afterwards. I had an interview at Compaq where they were 40 minutes late and one interviewer was pretty insulting with his questions. Like you, I was taught to smile and just get through it. I did, however, sharply correct him on his dismissal of my experience. They called me back and wanted to o hire me but needed me to fill out some preliminary forms.. However, I wasn't keen on working there after that interview. So, I returned their favor for keeping me waiting 40 minutes and said, sure, I'll get you that information to you! I never responded back.

deltas.product_description:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.
