prediction.review_text:
I'm excited to share my recent experience testing AI in a real coding project! 🚀 I used Claude to enhance some WPF canvas code for our development team, adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel. It was a learning journey—some iterations didn’t compile, and I found myself making manual tweaks, which often felt quicker than re-prompting AI. 

Here are my key takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- **Exploration**: AI helped me discover APIs and language features I hadn’t encountered before, which was a significant time saver.
- **Refactoring**: Be prepared to clean up the code. While AI can assist, it’s no substitute for adhering to good coding practices.
- **Understanding**: The real value emerged when I took the time to grasp the new concepts introduced. 

AI fits seamlessly into the early stages of problem-solving, helping to piece together solutions while expanding my knowledge. Once I had a clear understanding of what 'good' looked like, it became easier to refine the code and align it with our company standards. 

I’m curious—how have you integrated AI into your workflows? What insights have you gained? #AI #Coding #WPF #SoftwareEngineering

actual.review_text:
Often times, I saw AI is useful is setting up the boiler plate code which is starting from scratch as opposed to fitting into existing code base is still not there to its full potential as we expect AI to be at.

It is good at giving the entire context which I feel can significantly improve the coding time if I were to code on my own as mentioned above reducing time to research.

deltas.product_description:
I'm a little late to the party, but I finally put AI to the test on a real coding project.

The project is a support tool for our development team, so low risk if something went wrong. I asked Claude to improve some WPF canvas code by adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel, along with click events.

It took several iterations. Sometimes the code didn’t compile, sometimes I fixed the bugs myself, and sometimes I just made tweaks by hand (which was faster than re-prompting AI). In the end, the antenna looked good. The code was a bit messy, but then again, the original codebase wasn't exactly pretty either, so maybe the AI was just following our conventions. 

My takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- Great for exploration. It surfaced APIs and language features I hadn’t seen before.

- Not great for polish. Expect to refactor if you care about clean code.

- Less useful when you already know the solution. Typing has never been the bottleneck.

The biggest time saver was discovering parts of the WPF Canvas API I didn’t know. Normally, I’d have learned this more slowly through searches and documentation. Still, I believe it's important to take time to study those new concepts that AI introduces us to. Otherwise we aren't really qualified to evaluate the quality of what AI produces.

For me, AI fit neatly into the early phase of problem-solving: piecing together a solution while expanding my knowledge. Once I understood the problem and knew what "good" looked like, it was easier to take over, clean things up, and bring the code up to company standards. Once the solution became clear, prompting AI further felt less like engineering and more like pulling a slot machine lever.

I know I still have a lot to learn in this new programming paradigm. How does this line up with your experience?

prediction.review_text: How do we build habits that prevent quitting? For me, it started as a child: protect yesterday’s win, then raise the ceiling. My journey began with perfect attendance in school, which transformed into a commitment to innovation and excellence at Microsoft. Each small win became the foundation for the next challenge. Recently, I took this mindset into our hospitality venture, Kēsar Vineyards and Events, where we’re creating a unique wedding venue. This weekend, I shadowed a renowned event planner to elevate our craft further. I'm sharing this because I believe many of us seek habits that foster resilience. I treat yesterday’s small win as the floor, refusing to break it, and then I add one. If you want to try this for yourself, pick one behavior that matters, set the smallest version you can maintain, and track your progress. Let’s keep pushing those boundaries together! #CompoundingEffort #IntrinsicMotivation #ContinuousGrowth

actual.review_text: Absolutely love what you said !! Very well articulated and Congratulations on everything that you do. Many more to come !!

deltas.product_description:
How I built a habit that prevents quitting? 

I developed this as a child. Protect yesterday’s win, then raise the ceiling.

It began in school. Two months without an absence became a quarter, then a year. From sixth through tenth grade I did not miss a day. The streak made the choice each morning. Protect the win. Add one.

That rule shaped bigger moves. I chose engineering and left home after tenth grade to build my own path. Received first US patent, then added more. Same pattern reflected in other aspects. Thirteen years of clean driving record. Sixteen years at Microsoft with strong peer relationships and consistent recognition. The goal was not tenure. The goal was to continuously raise yesterday’s baseline. If 30 pushups is the edge, I do 31. One extra rep turns a ceiling into a floor.

Outside of work, Hospitality Entrepreneurship tested the pattern. Seven years ago we (Rahil and I) converted our garage into a studio and hosted our first guests. Then more condos. Then a vineyard stay. Now we are building Kēsar Vineyards and Events a specialized multi-day wedding venue. Same rule. Keep the last win alive. Stretch it slightly. Then add one to create new personal baseline. This weekend we drove 5hrs to shadow a renowned event and wedding planner during a live event and study the craft up close. New input. New higher standard.

I’m sharing because many of us want a habit that prevents quitting. This is mine. I treat yesterday’s small win as the floor. I refuse to break the floor. Then I add one. I rely on my system: small streaks that become identity, and identity that makes the next action non-negotiable. Tiny on any given day. Transformative across a season.

If you want to try it for 30 days: pick one behavior that matters, set the smallest version you can keep on your worst day, track one visible receipt, protect the floor, then add one. #CompoundingEffort #TheStreakIsTheStrategy #IntrinsicMotivation

My takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- Great for exploration. It surfaced APIs and language features I hadn’t seen before.

- Not great for polish. Expect to refactor if you care about clean code.

- Less useful when you already know the solution. Typing has never been the bottleneck.

The biggest time saver was discovering parts of the WPF Canvas API I didn’t know. Normally, I’d have learned this more slowly through searches and documentation. Still, I believe it's important to take time to study those new concepts that AI introduces us to. Otherwise we aren't really qualified to evaluate the quality of what AI produces.

For me, AI fit neatly into the early phase of problem-solving: piecing together a solution while expanding my knowledge. Once I understood the problem and knew what "good" looked like, it was easier to take over, clean things up, and bring the code up to company standards. Once the solution became clear, prompting AI further felt less like engineering and more like pulling a slot machine lever.

I know I still have a lot to learn in this new programming paradigm. How does this line up with your experience?

prediction.review_text:
How do we cultivate habits that help us persevere? For me, it all started with a simple rule: protect yesterday’s win and then raise the ceiling. My journey began in school with perfect attendance, which laid the groundwork for my commitment to innovation and excellence at Microsoft. Each small win became a stepping stone for the next challenge, and I owe much of my success to the incredible teams I've worked with along the way. 

Recently, I applied this mindset to our hospitality venture, Kēsar Vineyards and Events, where we’re crafting a unique wedding venue. This past weekend, I had the incredible opportunity to shadow a renowned event planner, learning invaluable insights to elevate our craft. 

I’m sharing this because I believe many of us are searching for habits that foster resilience and community. I treat yesterday’s small win as my floor, refusing to break it, and then I add one. If you want to try this for yourself, pick one behavior that matters, set the smallest version you can maintain, and track your progress. Together, let's keep pushing those boundaries and celebrating our collective wins! #CompoundingEffort #IntrinsicMotivation #CommunityGrowth

actual.review_text: Absolutely love what you said !! Very well articulated and Congratulations on everything that you do. Many more to come !!

deltas.product_description:
How I built a habit that prevents quitting? 

I developed this as a child. Protect yesterday’s win, then raise the ceiling.

It began in school. Two months without an absence became a quarter, then a year. From sixth through tenth grade I did not miss a day. The streak made the choice each morning. Protect the win. Add one.

That rule shaped bigger moves. I chose engineering and left home after tenth grade to build my own path. Received first US patent, then added more. Same pattern reflected in other aspects. Thirteen years of clean driving record. Sixteen years at Microsoft with strong peer relationships and consistent recognition. The goal was not tenure. The goal was to continuously raise yesterday’s baseline. If 30 pushups is the edge, I do 31. One extra rep turns a ceiling into a floor.

Outside of work, Hospitality Entrepreneurship tested the pattern. Seven years ago we (Rahil and I) converted our garage into a studio and hosted our first guests. Then more condos. Then a vineyard stay. Now we are building Kēsar Vineyards and Events a specialized multi-day wedding venue. Same rule. Keep the last win alive. Stretch it slightly. Then add one to create new personal baseline. This weekend we drove 5hrs to shadow a renowned event and wedding planner during a live event and study the craft up close. New input. New higher standard.

I’m sharing because many of us want a habit that prevents quitting. This is mine. I treat yesterday’s small win as the floor. I refuse to break the floor. Then I add one. I rely on my system: small streaks that become identity, and identity that makes the next action non-negotiable. Tiny on any given day. Transformative across a season.

If you want to try it for 30 days: pick one behavior that matters, set the smallest version you can keep on your worst day, track one visible receipt, protect the floor, then add one. #CompoundingEffort #TheStreakIsTheStrategy #IntrinsicMotivation

prediction.review_text:
As I step into my new role at Microsoft, I can't help but reflect on a past interview experience that taught me a valuable lesson. I encountered an interviewer who seemed more interested in asserting dominance than in understanding my perspective. When he asked about the differences between Java and C++, I provided a detailed response, only to be met with a loud 'WRONG!' and a claim that they were the same language. 

This interaction was a reminder of the importance of fostering respectful dialogue in our industry. It’s crucial that we create environments where curiosity and collaboration thrive, rather than one where arrogance stifles growth. 

To anyone navigating challenging interviews, remember: if you sense a negative vibe, trust your instincts. It's often a reflection of the work culture you might be stepping into. Let's uplift each other and ensure every voice is valued. Have you had similar experiences? I’d love to hear your stories and insights! #CareerDevelopment #Mentorship #Microsoft

actual.review_text: It's better to find out before you get hired than afterwards. I had an interview at Compaq where they were 40 minutes late and one interviewer was pretty insulting with his questions. Like you, I was taught to smile and just get through it. I did, however, sharply correct him on his dismissal of my experience. They called me back and wanted to o hire me but needed me to fill out some preliminary forms.. However, I wasn't keen on working there after that interview. So, I returned their favor for keeping me waiting 40 minutes and said, sure, I'll get you that information to you! I never responded back.

deltas.product_description:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.

prediction.review_text:
As I step into my role as a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft, I find myself reflecting on the importance of respectful dialogue in our industry, especially in the context of interviews. I once encountered an interviewer who seemed more interested in showcasing his own superiority than in understanding my qualifications. When he asked about the differences between Java and C++, I provided a detailed response, only to be met with a loud 'WRONG!' and an assertion that they were the same language. 

This experience taught me a valuable lesson: if an interview leaves you with a bad feeling, it’s often a sign of the work culture you might be entering. We need to foster environments where curiosity and collaboration thrive, rather than allowing arrogance to stifle growth. 

To anyone navigating challenging interviews, trust your instincts. If something feels off, it probably is. Let’s uplift each other and ensure every voice is valued in our professional journeys. Have you had similar experiences? I’d love to hear your stories! #CareerDevelopment #InterviewInsights #Microsoft

actual.review_text: It's better to find out before you get hired than afterwards. I had an interview at Compaq where they were 40 minutes late and one interviewer was pretty insulting with his questions. Like you, I was taught to smile and just get through it. I did, however, sharply correct him on his dismissal of my experience. They called me back and wanted to o hire me but needed me to fill out some preliminary forms.. However, I wasn't keen on working there after that interview. So, I returned their favor for keeping me waiting 40 minutes and said, sure, I'll get you that information to you! I never responded back.

deltas.product_description:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.

actual.review_text: Absolutely love what you said !! Very well articulated and Congratulations on everything that you do. Many more to come !!

deltas.product_description:
How I built a habit that prevents quitting? 

I developed this as a child. Protect yesterday’s win, then raise the ceiling.

It began in school. Two months without an absence became a quarter, then a year. From sixth through tenth grade I did not miss a day. The streak made the choice each morning. Protect the win. Add one.

That rule shaped bigger moves. I chose engineering and left home after tenth grade to build my own path. Received first US patent, then added more. Same pattern reflected in other aspects. Thirteen years of clean driving record. Sixteen years at Microsoft with strong peer relationships and consistent recognition. The goal was not tenure. The goal was to continuously raise yesterday’s baseline. If 30 pushups is the edge, I do 31. One extra rep turns a ceiling into a floor.

Outside of work, Hospitality Entrepreneurship tested the pattern. Seven years ago we (Rahil and I) converted our garage into a studio and hosted our first guests. Then more condos. Then a vineyard stay. Now we are building Kēsar Vineyards and Events a specialized multi-day wedding venue. Same rule. Keep the last win alive. Stretch it slightly. Then add one to create new personal baseline. This weekend we drove 5hrs to shadow a renowned event and wedding planner during a live event and study the craft up close. New input. New higher standard.

I’m sharing because many of us want a habit that prevents quitting. This is mine. I treat yesterday’s small win as the floor. I refuse to break the floor. Then I add one. I rely on my system: small streaks that become identity, and identity that makes the next action non-negotiable. Tiny on any given day. Transformative across a season.

If you want to try it for 30 days: pick one behavior that matters, set the smallest version you can keep on your worst day, track one visible receipt, protect the floor, then add one. #CompoundingEffort #TheStreakIsTheStrategy #IntrinsicMotivation

I finally took the plunge into AI-assisted coding, and it's been an eye-opening experience! Recently, I worked on a support tool for our development team, using Claude to enhance some WPF canvas code by adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel. 

The process was a journey of exploration and iteration. I encountered some compilation issues, and there were times when I found it faster to make manual tweaks than to re-prompt AI. Here are my key takeaways from this experience:

- **AI as a Collaborator**: It opened up new APIs and language features I hadn’t encountered before, significantly accelerating my learning curve.
- **Refactoring Required**: While AI can assist, it’s crucial to refine the output to maintain clean code standards.
- **Understanding is Key**: The real value came from taking the time to grasp the new concepts AI introduced. Without this understanding, we can't effectively evaluate the quality of AI-generated code. 

AI fits seamlessly into the early stages of problem-solving, helping to piece together solutions while expanding my knowledge. Once I had a clear vision of what 'good' looked like, it was much easier to refine the code to meet our company standards. 

I'm excited about the potential of AI in our workflows, but I know there's still a lot to learn in this evolving programming landscape. How have you integrated AI into your projects? What insights have you gained from your experiences? #AI #SoftwareEngineering #WPF #Collaboration

deltas.product_description:
I'm a little late to the party, but I finally put AI to the test on a real coding project.

The project is a support tool for our development team, so low risk if something went wrong. I asked Claude to improve some WPF canvas code by adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel, along with click events.

It took several iterations. Sometimes the code didn’t compile, sometimes I fixed the bugs myself, and sometimes I just made tweaks by hand (which was faster than re-prompting AI). In the end, the antenna looked good. The code was a bit messy, but then again, the original codebase wasn't exactly pretty either, so maybe the AI was just following our conventions. 

My takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- Great for exploration. It surfaced APIs and language features I hadn’t seen before.

- Not great for polish. Expect to refactor if you care about clean code.

- Less useful when you already know the solution. Typing has never been the bottleneck.

The biggest time saver was discovering parts of the WPF Canvas API I didn’t know. Normally, I’d have learned this more slowly through searches and documentation. Still, I believe it's important to take time to study those new concepts that AI introduces us to. Otherwise we aren't really qualified to evaluate the quality of what AI produces.

For me, AI fit neatly into the early phase of problem-solving: piecing together a solution while expanding my knowledge. Once I understood the problem and knew what "good" looked like, it was easier to take over, clean things up, and bring the code up to company standards. Once the solution became clear, prompting AI further felt less like engineering and more like pulling a slot machine lever.

I know I still have a lot to learn in this new programming paradigm. How does this line up with your experience?

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.

#Azure #AzureNetworking #Microsoft

actual.review_text: Rhyan Waine thanks for the feedback. We are always looking at ways to improve our products, and we will see if there is opportunity here to leverage this technology into other areas of the Azure networking ecosystem.

deltas.product_description:
Virtual Network Routing Appliance ("Routing Appliance") Public Preview!

During Ignite, I posted about exciting developments in building a high-performance, native appliance in Azure that will help customers achieve low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need of a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). Today I'm happy to announce that we have released a public preview of this feature. I hope you get a chance to try it out and we are looking to build many more exciting capabilities on top of this new technology.

NVAs have been the backbone of complex Azure networking topologies where customers needed to route traffic through a central point. While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point. NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps. You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

Read More: 

Thanks to Ashish Bhargava, Mario Lopez, Mohamed Hassan, Jackson Crewe, Nitin Goel, Pan Wang, Anshu Verma, Arjun Patel, Rishabh Tewari, Neeraj Motwani, Avijit Gupta, Damini Singal, Michal Zygmunt, Deepak Bansal

#azure #azurenetworking #microsoft

prediction.review_text:
I'm excited to share that the Network Security Perimeter (NSP) is now generally available! 🎉 It's been a privilege to collaborate with the NSP team, who have worked tirelessly to enhance Azure Virtual Network Manager. While this isn't my direct product, the close partnership we've built has allowed us to share ideas, code, and insights that truly elevate our offerings.

NSP is a game changer for organizations looking to define secure boundaries for their PaaS resources. Here are some key features:

- **Resource communication within the perimeter**: This prevents unauthorized data access, ensuring that sensitive information remains protected.
- **Explicit rules for external access**: Organizations can manage public access to their PaaS resources with precision.
- **Comprehensive access logs**: These are crucial for audit and compliance, providing transparency in operations.
- **Unified experience across PaaS resources**: This simplifies management and enhances usability.

A great example of NSP in action is with Azure Monitor, which can leverage these capabilities to enhance security and operational efficiency.

Kudos to the entire NSP team for their dedication and hard work! Let's continue to innovate and drive impactful solutions together. #Azure #NetworkSecurity #CloudComputing

deltas.product_description:
As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements. This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space. While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together. It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound. 

Features of a network security perimeter include: 
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations. 
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter. 
* Access logs for audit and compliance. 
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: 

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:

prediction.review_text:
Today marks a significant milestone for Azure Connectivity! 🚀 After years of dedication and collaboration, I'm excited to share that Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connectivity are now generally available. This innovation empowers our customers to seamlessly connect multiple virtual networks, eliminating the cumbersome process of individual peerings and complex route table management. In just minutes, hundreds of virtual networks can be linked with minimal latency, transforming cloud networking at scale.

I want to express my heartfelt gratitude to my incredible team. Your expertise, hard work, and collaborative spirit have made this vision a reality. This achievement is not just about launching a new product; it reflects our commitment to mentorship and professional growth within our team. Let's continue to support each other and push the boundaries of what’s possible in the cloud! #Azure #CloudNetworking #Mentorship #Teamwork

actual.review_text: Anders Spælling - Feedback is heard loud and clear. I'm working now with my business counterparts (Andrea Michael, etc) to determine ways to improve this.

deltas.product_description:
A great day for Connectivity in Azure! After years of effort, I'm pleased to announce that we have released Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connective to general availability.

Customers can now connect multiple virtual networks together with ease. In the past you would have to create virtual peerings for every connection, route tables, suffer additional hops and latency. Now, with Azure Virtual Network Manager, in a matter of minutes you can connect hundreds of virtual networks together with no additional hops, incredibly low latency, and without the need to create and manage route tables.

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.

prediction.review_text:
I'm thrilled to announce the public preview of the Virtual Network Routing Appliance! 🚀 This innovative Azure-native solution is set to revolutionize how we approach routing, delivering exceptional performance with speeds of up to 200 Gbps. 🌐

Reflecting on my journey, I've seen how Network Virtual Appliances (NVAs) often added unnecessary complexity for customers, requiring VM deployments and OS management. With the Routing Appliance, we simplify this process—achieving low-latency, high-throughput routing with just a few clicks. Plus, you can leverage Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) for seamless traffic management.

A heartfelt thank you to my amazing team—Ashish, Mario, Mohamed, Jackson, Nitin, Pan, Anshu, Arjun, Rishabh, Neeraj, Avijit, Damini, Michal, and Deepak—for their dedication and hard work in bringing this project to life. I'm excited to see how you all utilize this new capability to enhance your networking solutions!

Read more about it here: [link] 

#Azure #AzureNetworking #Microsoft

actual.review_text: Rhyan Waine thanks for the feedback. We are always looking at ways to improve our products, and we will see if there is opportunity here to leverage this technology into other areas of the Azure networking ecosystem.

deltas.product_description:
Virtual Network Routing Appliance ("Routing Appliance") Public Preview!

During Ignite, I posted about exciting developments in building a high-performance, native appliance in Azure that will help customers achieve low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need of a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). Today I'm happy to announce that we have released a public preview of this feature. I hope you get a chance to try it out and we are looking to build many more exciting capabilities on top of this new technology.

NVAs have been the backbone of complex Azure networking topologies where customers needed to route traffic through a central point. While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point. NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps. You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

Read More: 

Thanks to Ashish Bhargava, Mario Lopez, Mohamed Hassan, Jackson Crewe, Nitin Goel, Pan Wang, Anshu Verma, Arjun Patel, Rishabh Tewari, Neeraj Motwani, Avijit Gupta, Damini Singal, Michal Zygmunt, Deepak Bansal

#azure #azurenetworking #microsoft

Back in 2021 when I first began my pivot to data, there was only one online course I took:

- The lessons go beyond technical concepts, covering how to actually land your first data analytics job—from networking, to resume writing to interviewing skills

- A capstone at the end of the course allows you to reinforce the concepts via project-based learning

Coursera currently has an offer where you can try the Data Analytics Certificate or any other Professional Certificate from Google for just $1 for your first month (normally $59). So if you’re an aspiring data analyst with no idea where to begin--run, don’t walk 

This is a limited-time offer and details on how to redeem it will be in my comments go go go 

#dataanalytics #coursera #careers

prediction.review_text:
Today marks an exciting milestone as the Network Security Perimeter (NSP) is now generally available! 🎉 While this isn't my direct product, I’ve had the privilege of collaborating closely with the NSP team, sharing insights and code that have helped shape this innovative feature alongside Azure Virtual Network Manager. 

NSP is a game changer for organizations aiming to establish secure boundaries for their PaaS resources. Here are some key features that stand out:

- **Resource communication within the perimeter**: This ensures sensitive data remains protected from unauthorized access.
- **Explicit rules for external access**: Organizations can manage public access to their PaaS resources with precision, enhancing security.
- **Comprehensive access logs**: Essential for audit and compliance, these logs provide transparency and accountability in operations.
- **Unified experience across PaaS resources**: Simplifying management and enhancing usability for teams.

A great example of NSP in action is with Azure Monitor, which can leverage these capabilities to enhance security and operational efficiency.

Kudos to the NSP team for their hard work and dedication! Your commitment to innovation drives us forward in this ever-evolving tech landscape. Let’s continue to collaborate and create impactful solutions together! #Azure #NetworkSecurity #CloudComputing #Leadership #Collaboration

actual.review_text: David O'Brien I'm not fully familiar with the pricing model. I'll get back to you on that

deltas.product_description:
As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements. This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space. While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together. It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound. 

Features of a network security perimeter include: 
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations. 
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter. 
* Access logs for audit and compliance. 
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: 

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:

While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point. NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps. You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

Read More: 

Thanks to Ashish Bhargava, Mario Lopez, Mohamed Hassan, Jackson Crewe, Nitin Goel, Pan Wang, Anshu Verma, Arjun Patel, Rishabh Tewari, Neeraj Motwani, Avijit Gupta, Damini Singal, Michal Zygmunt, Deepak Bansal

#azure #azurenetworking #microsoft

prediction.review_text:
Today marks an exciting milestone as the Network Security Perimeter (NSP) is now generally available! 🎉 While this isn't my direct product, I've had the privilege of collaborating closely with the NSP team. Their hard work and innovative spirit have been inspiring as we've shared insights and code to shape this feature alongside Azure Virtual Network Manager.

NSP is a game changer for organizations aiming to establish secure boundaries for their PaaS resources. Here are some key features that stand out:

- **Resource communication within the perimeter**: This ensures sensitive data remains protected from unauthorized access.
- **Explicit rules for external access**: Organizations can manage public access to their PaaS resources with precision, enhancing security.
- **Comprehensive access logs**: Essential for audit and compliance, these logs provide transparency and accountability in operations.
- **Unified experience across PaaS resources**: Simplifying management and enhancing usability for teams.

A great example of NSP in action is with Azure Monitor, which can leverage these capabilities to enhance security and operational efficiency.

Kudos to the NSP team for their dedication and teamwork! Your commitment to innovation drives us forward in this ever-evolving tech landscape. Let's continue to collaborate and create impactful solutions together! #Azure #NetworkSecurity #CloudComputing #Collaboration #Leadership

actual.review_text: David O'Brien I'm not fully familiar with the pricing model. I'll get back to you on that

deltas.product_description:
As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements. This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space. While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together. It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound. 

Features of a network security perimeter include: 
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations. 
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter. 
* Access logs for audit and compliance. 
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: 

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:

deltas.product_description:
A great day for Connectivity in Azure! After years of effort, I'm pleased to announce that we have released Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connective to general availability.

Customers can now connect multiple virtual networks together with ease. In the past you would have to create virtual peerings for every connection, route tables, suffer additional hops and latency. Now, with Azure Virtual Network Manager, in a matter of minutes you can connect hundreds of virtual networks together with no additional hops, incredibly low latency, and without the need to create and manage route tables.

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.

deltas.product_description:
5 dokaza da je Rajder iz Patrolnih šapa toksični mikromenadžer:

1. Ne vrednuje tuđe vreme. Na sastanke saziva sve članove tima, umesto samo one koji će učestvovati u akciji.

2. Govori drugima šta da rade, umesto da njima to prepusti. Kao da Rabl ne zna šta treba da radi kašikom bagera ili Skaj kukom helikoptera.

3. Favorizuje određene članove tima. U ogromnom broju akcija učestvuju Čejs i Skaj, dok Maršal dođe na red u svakoj 10. epizodi (čekali smo pet dana da čujemo koja je Maršalova rečenica kad ga Rajder prozove)

4. Ne poštuje slobodno vreme članova svog tima. Često ih zove čak i po mraku, očekujući da se oni momentalno odazovu. To stvara ogroman stres kod Šapa.

5. Daje klijentima obećanja bez prethodne saglasnosti članova svog tima. Svakom ko ga nazove kaže da odmah dolaze i da će biti rešeno, čime Šapama ne ostavlja nimalo prostora za pregovore i stalno ih baca u vatru.

(Do temperature je, nije do mene)

prediction.review_text:
After 15 years in IT, I've witnessed the promise of open spaces to enhance collaboration, but the reality often tells a different story. 🎧 Instead of fostering communication, these environments can create barriers to focus and productivity. I often find myself wishing for better headphones and a quiet space to truly engage with my work. 

In my experience, collaboration thrives on trust and openness, not just proximity. If the organizational culture lacks these elements, simply removing walls won’t change anything. It’s crucial to prioritize building a culture of communication and trust over merely rearranging furniture. 

Let's reflect on how we can create environments that genuinely support our teams, rather than just following trends. How can we foster real connections and collaboration in our workplaces? 

#TeamDynamics #Collaboration #OrganizationalCulture

actual.review_text: U principu tačno. Teško je ukomponovati da ljudi koji rade zajedno baš sede jedni pored drugih, možda i nemoguče. Danas radim sa jednim kolegom i saradjujem dosta, a za dve nedelje će mi glavni saradnik biti neko potuno drugi.

deltas.product_description:
Zašto je open space sr…nje? Ili u prevodu, kada mogu da dobijem bolje slušalice?

Petnaest godina sam vodio IT kompanije.
Deset godina pre toga radio sam u njima, kao inženjer.
Za sve to vreme nagledao sam se različitih kancelarijskih formata, ali jedna tema se nike menjala, open space. Uvek je imao istu priču: povećaće kolaboraciju, ljudi će više razgovarati, biće otvoreniji jedni prema drugima i efikasnost rada na projektima će porasti.

Ali stvarnost je potpuno drugačija. Da li mogu da dobijem bolje slušalice i mikrofon koji izoluje buku, da li mogu da se premestim u drugu kancelariju, da li mogu intervju sa klijentom da odradim od kuće, potreban mi je fokus. 

U tim prostorima gde bi trebalo da se „svi čuju“, ljudi se sve manje slušaju.
Sede jedni pored drugih, a ne kažu ni „dobro jutro“. Kada sam prvi put to video davne 2008.godine, nisam mogao da verujem. Sedim u open space kancelariji za pedesetak ljudi, u drugom gradu, i gledam, ulaze jedan po jedan, i samo re retki javljaju jedni drugima. Oni koji sede jedni do drugih, naravno, najšešće.
Neki ne znaju imena svojih kolega, niti imaju predstavu ko uopšte radi sa kojim tehnologijama i na kojim projektima.
Zamišljen kao otvoren, zajednički prostor, open space se pretvorio u zatvorenost, u prostor gde se svako brani od drugih svojim slušalicama, ekranima i pogledom u pod.

Menadžment voli da kaže da zidovi prave barijere.
Istina je da barijere prave ljudi.
Ako ti je organizaciona kultura zatvorena, ako nema poverenja, ako ljudi ne osećaju potrebu da se diskutuju jedni sa drugima, skidanje zidova neće promeniti ništa osim troškova za molera.
I tu leži ironija: kada saradnja ne postoji, nije problem u rasporedu stolova, nego u onome kako vodiš firmu, i kakvu organizacionu kulturu živi ta kompanija. Ono što sam naučio kroz karijeru i kroz istraživanje u pripremi disertacije je da je organizaciona kultura kao voda. Ukoliko je ti ne oblikuješ, ne menadžuješ, sa stavom, “ma sve će to da se desi samo od sebe”, organizaciona kultura nadje svoj put, i svoj način da se uoblikuje. Često, ne u ono što menadžment kompanije želi.

Zašto se i dalje guramo u open space, zašto se to prodaje kao „rešenje za budućnost rada“, i zašto menadžment uporno brani koncept koji očigledno ne funkcioniše?
Možda zato što je lakše meriti kvadrate nego poverenje.
Ali pravo pitanje je: da li menadžment zaista veruje da ljudi rade bolje kada sede zajedno, ili je to samo mit koji niko ne sme da prizna da ne funkcioniše? Ja nisam smeo, priznajem.

Let’s reflect on how we can create environments that genuinely support our teams. How can we foster real connections and collaboration in our workplaces? I’d love to hear your thoughts!

#TeamDynamics #Collaboration #OrganizationalCulture

actual.review_text: U principu tačno. Teško je ukomponovati da ljudi koji rade zajedno baš sede jedni pored drugih, možda i nemoguče. Danas radim sa jednim kolegom i saradjujem dosta, a za dve nedelje će mi glavni saradnik biti neko potuno drugi.

deltas.product_description:
Zašto je open space sr…nje? Ili u prevodu, kada mogu da dobijem bolje slušalice?

Petnaest godina sam vodio IT kompanije.
Deset godina pre toga radio sam u njima, kao inženjer.
Za sve to vreme nagledao sam se različitih kancelarijskih formata, ali jedna tema se nike menjala, open space. Uvek je imao istu priču: povećaće kolaboraciju, ljudi će više razgovarati, biće otvoreniji jedni prema drugima i efikasnost rada na projektima će porasti.

Ali stvarnost je potpuno drugačija. Da li mogu da dobijem bolje slušalice i mikrofon koji izoluje buku, da li mogu da se premestim u drugu kancelariju, da li mogu intervju sa klijentom da odradim od kuće, potreban mi je fokus. 

U tim prostorima gde bi trebalo da se „svi čuju“, ljudi se sve manje slušaju.
Sede jedni pored drugih, a ne kažu ni „dobro jutro“. Kada sam prvi put to video davne 2008.godine, nisam mogao da verujem. Sedim u open space kancelariji za pedesetak ljudi, u drugom gradu, i gledam, ulaze jedan po jedan, i samo re retki javljaju jedni drugima. Oni koji sede jedni do drugih, naravno, najšešće.
Neki ne znaju imena svojih kolega, niti imaju predstavu ko uopšte radi sa kojim tehnologijama i na kojim projektima.
Zamišljen kao otvoren, zajednički prostor, open space se pretvorio u zatvorenost, u prostor gde se svako brani od drugih svojim slušalicama, ekranima i pogledom u pod.

Menadžment voli da kaže da zidovi prave barijere.
Istina je da barijere prave ljudi.
Ako ti je organizaciona kultura zatvorena, ako nema poverenja, ako ljudi ne osećaju potrebu da se diskutuju jedni sa drugima, skidanje zidova neće promeniti ništa osim troškova za molera.
I tu leži ironija: kada saradnja ne postoji, nije problem u rasporedu stolova, nego u onome kako vodiš firmu, i kakvu organizacionu kulturu živi ta kompanija. Ono što sam naučio kroz karijeru i kroz istraživanje u pripremi disertacije je da je organizaciona kultura kao voda. Ukoliko je ti ne oblikuješ, ne menadžuješ, sa stavom, “ma sve će to da se desi samo od sebe”, organizaciona kultura nadje svoj put, i svoj način da se uoblikuje. Često, ne u ono što menadžment kompanije želi.

Zašto se i dalje guramo u open space, zašto se to prodaje kao „rešenje za budućnost rada“, i zašto menadžment uporno brani koncept koji očigledno ne funkcioniše?
Možda zato što je lakše meriti kvadrate nego poverenje.
Ali pravo pitanje je: da li menadžment zaista veruje da ljudi rade bolje kada sede zajedno, ili je to samo mit koji niko ne sme da prizna da ne funkcioniše? Ja nisam smeo, priznajem.

deltas.product_description:
A great day for Connectivity in Azure! After years of effort, I'm pleased to announce that we have released Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connective to general availability.

Customers can now connect multiple virtual networks together with ease. In the past you would have to create virtual peerings for every connection, route tables, suffer additional hops and latency. Now, with Azure Virtual Network Manager, in a matter of minutes you can connect hundreds of virtual networks together with no additional hops, incredibly low latency, and without the need to create and manage route tables.

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.

prediction.review_text:
Reflecting on team dynamics, I've been thinking about the impact of micromanagement on our productivity and morale. Here are five signs that might indicate you're dealing with a micromanager:

1. **Unnecessary Meetings**: Inviting everyone to meetings without a clear agenda can waste valuable time that could be better spent on actual work.
2. **Lack of Trust**: Dictating tasks instead of trusting team members to leverage their expertise stifles creativity and innovation. We all have unique skills that can drive our projects forward.
3. **Favoritism**: Favoring certain individuals can create unnecessary tension and feelings of exclusion within the team. It’s crucial to ensure everyone feels valued.
4. **Ignoring Personal Time**: Expecting immediate responses outside of work hours can lead to burnout. Respecting each other's time is vital for maintaining a healthy work-life balance.
5. **Breaking Trust**: Making promises to clients without consulting the team undermines trust and collaboration. We need to work together to set realistic expectations.

Let’s strive to cultivate a culture of empowerment, where every team member feels valued and respected. How do you ensure your team feels supported? #TeamDynamics #Leadership #Collaboration

actual.review_text: Ognjen Sobajic s druge strane, opet da ne bude da ga samo kudimo, uvek mi se svidjalo kako ocas posla napravi dobru prezentaciju gde je sve odlicno objasnjeno sa veoma lepim vizualizacijama. Verovatno je koristio neke napredne AI tool-ove jos tada.

deltas.product_description:
5 dokaza da je Rajder iz Patrolnih šapa toksični mikromenadžer:

1. Ne vrednuje tuđe vreme. Na sastanke saziva sve članove tima, umesto samo one koji će učestvovati u akciji.

2. Govori drugima šta da rade, umesto da njima to prepusti. Kao da Rabl ne zna šta treba da radi kašikom bagera ili Skaj kukom helikoptera.

3. Favorizuje određene članove tima. U ogromnom broju akcija učestvuju Čejs i Skaj, dok Maršal dođe na red u svakoj 10. epizodi (čekali smo pet dana da čujemo koja je Maršalova rečenica kad ga Rajder prozove)

4. Ne poštuje slobodno vreme članova svog tima. Često ih zove čak i po mraku, očekujući da se oni momentalno odazovu. To stvara ogroman stres kod Šapa.

5. Daje klijentima obećanja bez prethodne saglasnosti članova svog tima. Svakom ko ga nazove kaže da odmah dolaze i da će biti rešeno, čime Šapama ne ostavlja nimalo prostora za pregovore i stalno ih baca u vatru.

(Do temperature je, nije do mene)

Reflecting on team dynamics, I find it crucial to address the impact of micromanagement. Here are five signs indicating a micromanager in action:

1. **Unnecessary Meetings**: Inviting the entire team without a clear agenda can waste valuable time. Let's focus on involving only those who will actively contribute.
2. **Lack of Trust**: When leaders dictate tasks instead of empowering team members, it stifles creativity. We all have unique skills that can drive our projects forward.
3. **Favoritism**: Favoring certain individuals can create tension and feelings of exclusion. Every team member deserves to feel valued.
4. **Ignoring Personal Time**: Expecting immediate responses outside of work hours can lead to burnout. Respecting each other's time is essential for a healthy work-life balance.
5. **Breaking Trust**: Making promises to clients without team consultation undermines collaboration. We need to set realistic expectations together.

Let's strive for a culture of empowerment and collaboration, where every team member feels respected and valued. How do you ensure your team feels supported? #TeamDynamics #Leadership #Collaboration

deltas.product_description:
5 dokaza da je Rajder iz Patrolnih šapa toksični mikromenadžer:

1. Ne vrednuje tuđe vreme. Na sastanke saziva sve članove tima, umesto samo one koji će učestvovati u akciji.

2. Govori drugima šta da rade, umesto da njima to prepusti. Kao da Rabl ne zna šta treba da radi kašikom bagera ili Skaj kukom helikoptera.

3. Favorizuje određene članove tima. U ogromnom broju akcija učestvuju Čejs i Skaj, dok Maršal dođe na red u svakoj 10. epizodi (čekali smo pet dana da čujemo koja je Maršalova rečenica kad ga Rajder prozove)

4. Ne poštuje slobodno vreme članova svog tima. Često ih zove čak i po mraku, očekujući da se oni momentalno odazovu. To stvara ogroman stres kod Šapa.

5. Daje klijentima obećanja bez prethodne saglasnosti članova svog tima. Svakom ko ga nazove kaže da odmah dolaze i da će biti rešeno, čime Šapama ne ostavlja nimalo prostora za pregovore i stalno ih baca u vatru.

(Do temperature je, nije do mene)

deltas.product_description:
A great day for Connectivity in Azure! After years of effort, I'm pleased to announce that we have released Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connective to general availability.

Customers can now connect multiple virtual networks together with ease. In the past you would have to create virtual peerings for every connection, route tables, suffer additional hops and latency. Now, with Azure Virtual Network Manager, in a matter of minutes you can connect hundreds of virtual networks together with no additional hops, incredibly low latency, and without the need to create and manage route tables.

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.
